questions.q: Which cosmic function does Brahma fulfill within the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: In the Puranas Brahma is the creator (srishti) of the Trimurti, alongside Vishnu the preserver and Shiva the destroyer.

questions.q: How many faces is Brahma traditionally depicted with in Puranic iconography?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Brahma as four-faced (chaturmukha), his faces traditionally associated with the four directions and the four Vedas.

questions.q: Who is the consort of Brahma in Hindu tradition?
questions.explanation: Brahma's consort is Saraswati, the goddess of knowledge, music, and speech.

questions.q: What is the vahana (mount) of Brahma?
questions.explanation: Brahma's vahana is the hamsa, the swan or goose, a symbol of discernment and purity.

questions.q: Upon what does Brahma sit in his classic Puranic depiction?
questions.explanation: Brahma is seated upon a lotus, which in the Puranas rises from the navel of Vishnu.

questions.q: Which famous temple in Rajasthan is among the rare shrines dedicated to Brahma?
questions.explanation: The temple at Pushkar in Rajasthan is among the very few shrines dedicated to Brahma.

questions.q: How many arms is Brahma usually shown with in the Puranas?
questions.explanation: Brahma is traditionally depicted as four-armed, each hand bearing one of his sacred attributes.

questions.q: Saraswati, the consort of Brahma, is the goddess of what?
questions.explanation: Saraswati is venerated as the goddess of knowledge, learning, music, and speech.

questions.q: Which four sacred texts are said to be brought forth through Brahma at the dawn of each cosmic cycle?
questions.explanation: Through Brahma the worlds and the four Vedas are brought forth at the beginning of each cosmic cycle.

questions.q: From whose navel does the lotus bearing Brahma rise in Puranic imagery?
questions.explanation: In the Puranas, Brahma is born upon a lotus that rises from the navel of the reclining Vishnu.

questions.q: Which of these is one of the four objects Brahma is described as holding in the Puranas?
questions.explanation: Brahma is described holding the Vedas, a water pot (kamandalu), a rosary, and a ladle in his four hands.

questions.q: The rosary that Brahma holds is generally understood to represent what?
options: The counting of cosmic time and prayer
questions.explanation: The rosary (akshamala) held by Brahma symbolizes the cycle of time and the practice of meditation and recitation.

questions.q: Brahma's vahana, the hamsa, is celebrated in tradition for its symbolic ability to do what?
questions.explanation: The hamsa is traditionally praised for its power to separate milk from water, symbolizing the discernment of truth from illusion.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the periodic dissolution of the cosmos at the very end of Brahma's life is known as what?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe the maha-pralaya, the great dissolution, as occurring at the close of Brahma's life, after which all returns to the unmanifest.

questions.q: The Saptarishi, the seven great seers, are described in the Puranas as which relation to Brahma?
questions.explanation: The seven seers (Saptarishi) are described as the mind-born sons of Brahma who anchor cosmic order across each age.

questions.q: The wandering sage Narada, who carries his veena through the three worlds, is described in the Puranas as a son of which deity?
questions.explanation: Narada is described as a mind-born son of Brahma and a supreme devotee of Vishnu.

questions.q: In the story of Hiranyakashipu, from which deity did the tyrant win the boon of near-invincibility?
questions.explanation: According to the Bhagavata Purana, Hiranyakashipu won his boon of near-invincibility from Brahma through severe austerities.

questions.q: In the Ramayana, Kumbhakarna's request to Brahma was garbled by which goddess, condemning him to long sleep?
questions.explanation: The Ramayana relates that Saraswati altered Kumbhakarna's speech so his request to Brahma became one for prolonged sleep.

questions.q: The divine missile that never misses its mark and is presided over by Brahma is known as what?
questions.explanation: The Brahmastra is the supremely destructive divine missile presided over by Brahma, invoked by sacred mantra.

questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, Brahma's abode is placed at the summit of which cosmic mountain?
questions.explanation: The Puranas place Brahma's abode at the summit of Mount Meru, the golden axis of the universe.

questions.q: Ravana, the ten-headed king of Lanka, is described in the Ramayana as a grandson of which creator deity?
questions.explanation: The Ramayana describes Ravana as the son of the sage Vishrava and grandson of Brahma.

questions.q: Within the Trimurti, which deity is paired with Brahma as the preserver of the cosmos?
questions.explanation: In the Trimurti, Brahma the creator is paired with Vishnu the preserver and Shiva the destroyer.

questions.q: The ladle held by Brahma in his iconography is most directly associated with which sacred practice?
questions.explanation: The ladle (sruva) held by Brahma is the implement of the yajna, the fire sacrifice, linking him to Vedic ritual.

questions.q: According to the Lingodbhava myth associated with the Shiva Lingam, what did Brahma attempt and fail to do?
options: To find the upper end of an infinite pillar of light
questions.explanation: In the Lingodbhava myth, Shiva appeared as an infinite pillar of light whose ends neither Brahma nor Vishnu could find.

questions.q: In the Puranas, the rare worship of Brahma is sometimes explained by a curse said to have been pronounced upon him by which figure?
options: The sage Bhrigu or, in some accounts, his consort
questions.explanation: Puranic accounts attribute Brahma's scant worship to a curse, variously ascribed in different tellings, explaining why his shrines are so few.

questions.q: A single day of Brahma in Puranic time-reckoning is known as what span?
questions.explanation: A single day of Brahma is called a kalpa, an immense span during which creation endures before dissolution.

questions.q: The four Vedas are traditionally associated with the four faces of Brahma. Which is counted among them?
questions.explanation: The four Vedas associated with Brahma's faces are the Rigveda, Yajurveda, Samaveda, and Atharvaveda.

questions.q: In the Puranic image of Vishnu reclining on Shesha, what specifically marks the moment of Brahma's birth to begin a new creation?
options: The blowing of the conch Panchajanya
The churning of the ocean of milk
A lotus rising from Vishnu's navel
questions.explanation: From a lotus rising out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma is born to begin a new creation as Vishnu reclines on Shesha.

questions.q: Brahma is most directly identified with which of the abstract principles of Hindu thought?
options: The creative aspect of the one Brahman
questions.explanation: Brahma personifies the creative function of the one supreme Brahman expressed through the threefold cosmic activity.

questions.q: The Pushkar temple of Brahma stands beside which feature held sacred in its associated lore?
questions.explanation: The Brahma temple at Pushkar stands beside the sacred Pushkar lake, central to the pilgrimage site's tradition.
